preamble.summary: A new voice enters the drama. Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite, a younger man who has been listening in furious silence, can no longer contain himself. The narrator explains in prose (vv. 1-5) that the three friends have stopped answering Job because he is righteous in his own eyes, and that Elihu's anger burns against Job for justifying himself rather than God, and against the three friends for condemning Job without finding an answer. When Elihu finally speaks (vv. 6-22), his opening poem is entirely about the right to speak. He defers to age, then claims that age alone does not guarantee wisdom — it is the spirit in a person, the breath of the Almighty, that gives understanding. He watched the three friends fail, and now he is bursting like a wineskin full of new wine. He will speak without favoritism, without flattery, because his Maker would take him away if he did.
preamble.remarkable: Elihu is the most controversial character in the book of Job. He appears without introduction in the prologue, speaks for six chapters without interruption, and is never mentioned in God's response or the epilogue. Some scholars consider his speeches a later addition; others see him as the author's deliberate bridge between the failed arguments of the friends and the divine speeches of chapters 38-41. What is undeniable is the literary brilliance of his entrance: he is young, angry, and overflowing with words. His self-description as a wineskin about to burst (v. 19) is one of the most vivid images of compulsive speech in all of literature. He is not wrong that the friends have failed — they have. He is not wrong that age does not guarantee wisdom — it does not. But his extraordinary preamble about his own right to speak (sixteen verses of it) reveals a man who is more confident in his authority than in his argument. The poem is about Elihu, not about God or Job.
preamble.friction: The prose introduction (vv. 1-5) is dense with the word charah ('burned with anger') — it appears four times in five verses. Elihu is angry at Job for self-justification and angry at the friends for failing to answer. This double anger is the key to his character: he rejects both positions. He will not side with Job against God or with the friends against Job. He claims a third way. The question the reader must carry through the next four chapters is whether Elihu actually delivers on this promise or merely restates the friends' theology in younger, more eloquent packaging. The Hebrew root ruach ('spirit, wind, breath') dominates the poem — Elihu claims the ruach of God in him (v. 8), says his belly is like wine without a vent (v. 19), and must open his lips to find ruach ('relief,' literally 'wind,' v. 20). The spirit that inspires him is also the pressure that threatens to explode him.
preamble.connections: Elihu's claim that the spirit of God gives understanding (v. 8) echoes Proverbs 2:6 ('the LORD gives wisdom') and anticipates God's own speeches, where wisdom belongs to the Creator alone. His wineskin image (v. 19) prefigures Jesus' saying about new wine in old wineskins (Mark 2:22). The genealogy 'Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite, of the family of Ram' (v. 2) is unusually detailed — Buz is Nahor's son (Genesis 22:21), making Elihu a relative of Abraham. The name Barachel means 'God has blessed,' and Elihu means 'He is my God,' both theologically loaded names for a character about to lecture on divine justice.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּשְׁבְּת֞וּ שְׁלֹ֤שֶׁת הָאֲנָשִׁים֙ הָאֵ֔לֶּה מֵעֲנ֖וֹת אֶת־אִיּ֑וֹב כִּ֥י ה֖וּא צַדִּ֥יק בְּעֵינָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: So these three men ceased to answer Job, because he was righteous in his own eyes.
verses.rendering: These three men gave up responding to Job, for he considered himself righteous.
verses.expanded_rendering: The prose narrator breaks into the poetic dialogue to set the scene for a new character. The verb vayyishbetu ('they ceased, they stopped') from shavat — the same root as Shabbat — signals a complete halt, not a pause. The three friends have run out of arguments. The reason given is devastating in its simplicity: ki hu tsaddiq be'einav ('because he was righteous in his own eyes'). The narrator does not say Job was wrong to consider himself righteous — the prologue has already confirmed his righteousness (1:1, 1:8, 2:3). The problem is that the friends cannot break through Job's self-justification, and they have no new arguments to offer. They are silenced not by Job's logic but by his immovability.
translator_notes: This verse is prose narration, not poetry. The three friends (Eliphaz, Bildad, Zophar) have completed their cycles of argument. The phrase tsaddiq be'einav ('righteous in his own eyes') is ambiguous — it could be the narrator's subtle criticism of Job's self-righteousness, or simply an observation that Job maintained his innocence. Given the prologue's endorsement of Job's righteousness, the latter reading is more likely.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּ֤חַר אַ֨ף ׀ אֱלִיה֣וּא בֶן־בַּרַכְאֵ֣ל הַבּוּזִ֗י מִמִּשְׁפַּ֫חַ֥ת רָ֥ם בְּאִיּ֥וֹב חָרָ֣ה אַפּ֑וֹ עַֽל־צַדְּק֥וֹ נַ֝פְשׁ֗וֹ מֵאֱלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Then was kindled the wrath of Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite, of the kindred of Ram: against Job was his wrath kindled, because he justified himself rather than God.
verses.rendering: Then the anger of Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite, of the clan of Ram, burned hot. His anger burned against Job because he justified himself rather than God.
verses.expanded_rendering: The new character receives the fullest genealogical introduction in the book. Elihu ('He is my God') ben Barachel ('son of God-has-blessed') ha-Buzi ('the Buzite') mi-mishpachat Ram ('of the clan of Ram'). Four generations of identity, establishing Elihu as a man of known lineage, not a wanderer or stranger. The verb charah af ('his anger burned') appears here for the first of four times in this introduction — Elihu enters the book white-hot with rage. The cause: al tsaddeqo nafsho me-Elohim ('because he declared himself righteous rather than God'). Job, in Elihu's view, has been so busy defending his own innocence that he has implicitly accused God of injustice. Elihu's objection is theological, not personal: if Job is righteous, then God must be wrong, and Elihu will not permit that conclusion.
translator_notes: Buz is listed as a son of Nahor, Abraham's brother, in Genesis 22:21, making Elihu a distant relative of the Abrahamic family. The clan of Ram may connect to the Judahite genealogy of Ruth 4:19, though this is uncertain. The phrase tsaddeqo nafsho me-Elohim ('he justified himself more than God') could also be translated 'he justified himself before God' — but the comparative sense is stronger: Job made himself righteous at God's expense.

key_terms.semantic_range: to burn, to be kindled, to blaze with anger; af = nostril, face, anger
key_terms.note: charah af ('his nostril burned') is the Hebrew Bible's most visceral idiom for anger. The af ('nostril') swells and heats; rage is experienced as a physical burning in the face. This phrase appears four times in five verses (vv. 2, 3, 5), making anger the defining characteristic of Elihu's entrance. He does not arrive with wisdom or comfort — he arrives on fire.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבִשְׁלֹ֤שֶׁת רֵעָ֗יו חָרָ֣ה אַפּ֑וֹ עַ֤ל אֲשֶׁ֥ר לֹא־מָצְא֥וּ מַ֝עֲנֶ֗ה וַיַּרְשִׁ֥יעוּ אֶת־אִיּֽוֹב׃
verses.text_kjv: Also against his three friends was his wrath kindled, because they had found no answer, and yet had condemned Job.
verses.rendering: His anger also burned against his three friends, because they had found no answer and yet had condemned Job.
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu's rage is bipartisan. He is angry at Job for self-justification, and now the same verb charah af fires against the three friends for the opposite failure: lo matse'u ma'aneh ('they did not find an answer'). They spoke for three cycles, marshaling tradition, experience, and logic, and failed to refute Job. Worse, despite finding no answer, vayyarshi'u et Iyov ('they declared Job guilty'). The Hiphil of rasha ('to declare wicked, to condemn') is a legal term — the friends rendered a guilty verdict without evidence. They convicted Job by theological deduction rather than factual argument. Elihu sees this as intellectual and moral failure: if you cannot answer a man, you have no right to condemn him.
translator_notes: The tension between 'found no answer' and 'condemned Job' is the crux of Elihu's critique of the friends. They operated from a closed system: suffering proves guilt, therefore Job is guilty. But they never demonstrated what Job's guilt was. Elihu will attempt what they could not — to explain suffering without accusing the sufferer.

verses.text_hebrew: וֶאֱלִיה֗וּ חִכָּ֣ה אֶת־אִ֭יּוֹב בִּדְבָרִ֑ים כִּ֤י זְקֵנִ֖ים הֵ֣מָּה מִמֶּ֣נּוּ לְיָמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Now Elihu had waited till Job had spoken, because they were elder than he.
verses.rendering: Now Elihu had waited to speak to Job, because they were older than he in years.
translator_notes: The verb chikkah ('he waited') indicates deliberate restraint, not passive silence. Elihu held back because of age-based deference: zeqenim hemmah mimmennu le-yamim ('they were elder than he in days'). Ancient Near Eastern culture demanded that younger men yield the floor to elders. Elihu has been observing protocol, but his patience has reached its limit.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּ֤רְא אֱלִיה֗וּא כִּ֘י אֵ֤ין מַעֲנֶ֗ה בְּפִ֛י שְׁלֹ֥שֶׁת הָאֲנָשִׁ֖ים וַיִּ֥חַר אַפּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: When Elihu saw that there was no answer in the mouth of these three men, then his wrath was kindled.
verses.rendering: When Elihu saw that there was no answer in the mouths of these three men, his anger burned.
translator_notes: The fourth appearance of charah af in this introduction. The phrase ein ma'aneh be-fi ('there was no answer in the mouth of') conveys total rhetorical failure — the friends' mouths are empty. Their arguments have been exhausted. This observation triggers Elihu's final ignition: he has waited long enough, and no one else is going to answer. The prose introduction ends here; poetry begins in verse 6.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּ֤עַן אֱלִיה֣וּא בֶן־בַּרַכְאֵ֣ל הַבּוּזִ֗י וַיֹּ֫אמַ֥ר צָ֘עִ֤יר אֲנִ֣י לְ֭יָמִים וְאַתֶּ֣ם יְשִׁישִׁ֑ים עַל־כֵּ֥ן זָ֝חַ֗לְתִּי וָאִ֥רָא מֵחַוֹּ֥ת דֵּ֝עִ֗י אֶתְכֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite answered and said, I am young, and ye are very old; wherefore I was afraid, and durst not shew you mine opinion.
verses.rendering:
Then Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite answered and said:
I am young in years, and you are aged;
therefore I held back, I was afraid
to declare what I know to you.
verses.expanded_rendering: The poetry begins. Elihu opens with a confession of social position: tsa'ir ani le-yamim ('I am small in days') — young, junior, the least qualified to speak in this company. The contrast is ve-attem yeshishim ('and you are aged, grey-haired'). The verb zachalti ('I crept back, I shrank, I was afraid') from zachal conveys physical recoiling — Elihu literally shrank from the conversation, making himself smaller. The verb va-ira ('and I feared') reinforces the point. The phrase me-chavvot de'i etkhem ('from declaring my knowledge to you') uses chavvot ('to declare, to show'), a rare word suggesting formal presentation. Elihu is saying: I had knowledge but was afraid to present it before my elders. This is not false modesty — it is the genuine position of youth in a gerontocratic culture. But the speech that follows will systematically dismantle the authority of age.
translator_notes: The word yeshishim ('very old, aged') occurs only here and in 15:10 in Job. It implies not just old age but venerable antiquity. Elihu's deference is culturally appropriate but will soon be qualified: age deserves first hearing, not final authority.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֭מַרְתִּי יָמִ֣ים יְדַבֵּ֑רוּ וְרֹ֥ב שָׁ֝נִ֗ים יֹדִ֥יעוּ חׇכְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: I said, Days should speak, and multitude of years should teach wisdom.
verses.rendering:
I thought, 'Let days speak,
and the abundance of years teach wisdom.'
translator_notes: Elihu articulates the conventional principle he has been following: yamim yedabberu ('days should speak') — age has the right to speak first. The parallel ve-rov shanim yodi'u chokhmah ('and abundance of years should make wisdom known') states the assumption: more years equals more wisdom. Elihu presents this as his former belief, which he is about to overturn.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֭כֵן רֽוּחַ־הִ֣יא בֶאֱנ֑וֹשׁ וְנִשְׁמַ֖ת שַׁדַּ֣י תְּבִינֵֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: But there is a spirit in man, and the inspiration of the Almighty giveth them understanding.
verses.rendering:
But it is the spirit in a person,
the breath of the Almighty, that gives understanding.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is Elihu's theological pivot. The word akhen ('surely, but indeed') overturns the principle of verse 7. Wisdom does not come from accumulated years but from ruach ('spirit') — the same word used for God's creative breath in Genesis 1:2 and for the animating force that makes humans alive (Genesis 2:7). The parallel line identifies this spirit: nishmat Shaddai ('the breath of the Almighty'). The verb tevinem ('gives them understanding') from bin ('to discern, to understand') means that comprehension is a divine gift, not an age-based achievement. Elihu is not claiming personal genius — he is claiming that God distributes wisdom by spirit, not by seniority. A young man with God's spirit outranks an old man without it. This is a radical democratization of wisdom.
translator_notes: The pairing of ruach ('spirit, wind, breath') and neshamah ('breath, life-breath') echoes Genesis 2:7 where God breathes the neshamah of life into Adam. Elihu's argument is that the same divine breath that gives life also gives understanding. The name Shaddai ('the Almighty') is Job's preferred name for God throughout the dialogue — Elihu adopts Job's own theological vocabulary.

key_terms.semantic_range: wind, breath, spirit, mind, disposition, the animating force of life
key_terms.note: ruach is the key word of Elihu's opening speech. It means 'spirit' (v. 8), 'wind' (v. 18, the pressure in his belly), and 'relief' (v. 20, the release he needs). Elihu's argument is built on a wordplay: the ruach that gives wisdom is the same ruach that compels him to speak. He cannot hold it in — the spirit of God is both his authority and his urgency.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־רַבִּ֥ים יֶחְכָּ֑מוּ וּ֝זְקֵנִ֗ים יָבִ֥ינוּ מִשְׁפָּֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: Great men are not always wise: neither do the aged understand judgment.
verses.rendering:
It is not the great who are wise,
nor the aged who understand justice.
translator_notes: The lo rabbim yechkamu ('not the many/great are wise') directly challenges the assumption of verse 7. The parallel u-zeqenim yavinu mishpat ('and elders understand judgment') with the implied negative extends the challenge: neither numbers nor years guarantee wisdom or just discernment. The word mishpat ('judgment, justice, right ruling') is a legal term — Elihu is saying the elders have failed to render a correct verdict in Job's case.

verses.text_hebrew: לָכֵ֣ן אָ֭מַרְתִּי שִׁמְעָ֣ה לִּ֑י אֲחַוֶּ֖ה דֵּ֝עִ֗י אַף־אָֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Therefore I said, Hearken to me; I also will shew mine opinion.
verses.rendering:
Therefore I say: Listen to me.
I too will declare what I know.
translator_notes: Having established that age does not guarantee wisdom and that the spirit of God gives understanding to anyone, Elihu now claims his right to speak: shim'ah li ('listen to me'). The achavveh de'i af ani ('I too will declare my knowledge') uses the same verb chavvah from verse 6, but now without the fear. The af ani ('I also, even I') is emphatic — Elihu inserts himself into the conversation as a legitimate participant.

verses.text_hebrew: הֵ֤ן הוֹחַ֗לְתִּי לְדִבְרֵיכֶ֥ם אָ֭זִין עַד־תְּב֣וּנֹתֵיכֶ֑ם עַד־תַּחְקְר֥וּן מִלִּֽין׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, I waited for your words; I gave ear to your reasons, whilst ye searched out what to say.
verses.rendering:
Look — I waited for your words.
I listened for your insights
while you searched for what to say.
translator_notes: The verb hochalti ('I waited, I hoped') implies patient, expectant waiting — Elihu genuinely hoped the elders would produce a compelling answer. The azin ('I gave ear') conveys attentive listening. The phrase ad tachqerun millin ('until you searched out words') uses chaqar ('to search, to probe, to investigate') — a word used for mining precious metals. Elihu watched them dig for arguments. They came up empty.

verses.text_hebrew: וְעָדֵיכֶ֣ם אֶתְבּוֹנָ֑ן וְהִנֵּ֤ה אֵ֖ין לְאִיּ֣וֹב מוֹכִ֑יחַ עוֹנֶ֖ה אֲמָרָ֣יו מִכֶּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, I attended unto you, and, behold, there was none of you that convinced Job, or that answered his words:
verses.rendering:
I paid close attention to you,
but not one of you refuted Job.
None of you answered his arguments.
translator_notes: The verb etbonan ('I paid close attention, I discerned carefully') from bin intensifies the listening — Elihu was not passively hearing but actively evaluating. The verdict: ein le-Iyov mokhiach ('there is no one proving Job wrong'). The word mokhiach (Hiphil participle of yakach, 'to reprove, to argue, to decide') is a legal term for a successful prosecution. No one successfully prosecuted the case against Job. The parallel oneh amarav ('answering his words') confirms the failure: Job's arguments stand unanswered.

verses.text_hebrew: פֶּן־תֹּ֣אמְר֔וּ מָצָ֖אנוּ חׇכְמָ֑ה אֵ֖ל יִדְּפֶ֥נּוּ לֹא־אִֽישׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: Lest ye should say, We have found out wisdom: God thrusteth him down, not man.
verses.rendering:
Do not say, 'We have found wisdom —
only God can refute him, not a man.'
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu anticipates the friends' excuse: pen tomeru matsa'nu chokhmah ('lest you say, we have found wisdom'). The friends might claim that their failure is actually a discovery — they have discovered that only God can answer Job. But Elihu rejects this retreat: El yiddefennu lo ish ('God will drive him out, not a man') is not an acceptable conclusion. The friends cannot delegate the argument to God and claim credit for theological wisdom. Elihu insists that a human answer is both possible and necessary. He will provide it. This verse is crucial for understanding Elihu's self-understanding: he believes he can do what the friends could not and what they wrongly claim only God can do.
translator_notes: The verb yiddefennu ('will thrust him, will drive him away') from nadaf suggests forceful pushing. The friends' position — that only God's direct intervention can answer Job — is exactly what will happen in chapters 38-41. But Elihu does not know that yet. He believes the human case can be made.

verses.text_hebrew: וְלֹא־עָרַ֣ךְ אֵלַ֣י מִלִּ֑ין וּ֝בְאִמְרֵיכֶ֗ם לֹ֣א אֲשִׁיבֶֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Now he hath not directed his words against me: neither will I answer him with your speeches.
verses.rendering:
He has not directed his words against me,
and I will not answer him with your arguments.
translator_notes: Elihu makes two claims: first, Job has not addressed him personally, so Elihu approaches without personal offense. Second, and more importantly, u-ve-imreikhem lo ashivennu ('and with your words I will not answer him') — Elihu will not recycle the friends' failed arguments. He promises a fresh approach, not a repackaging of retribution theology. Whether he keeps this promise is one of the central interpretive questions of the Elihu speeches.

verses.text_hebrew: חַ֭תּוּ לֹא־עָ֣נוּ ע֑וֹד הֶעְתִּ֖יקוּ מֵהֶ֣ם מִלִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: They were amazed, they answered no more: they left off speaking.
verses.rendering:
They are dismayed; they answer no more.
Words have departed from them.
translator_notes: Elihu now addresses the audience (or perhaps speaks to himself) about the friends in the third person. The verb chattu ('they were dismayed, they were shattered') from chatat conveys defeat. The phrase he'etiqu mehem millin ('words have moved away from them') is a vivid image — words themselves have abandoned the three friends. Language has left the building. Elihu steps into the verbal vacuum.

verses.text_hebrew: וְ֭הוֹחַלְתִּי כִּי־לֹ֣א יְדַבֵּ֑רוּ כִּ֥י עָ֝מְד֗וּ לֹא־עָ֥נוּ עֽוֹד׃
verses.text_kjv: When I had waited, (for they spake not, but stood still, and answered no more;)
verses.rendering:
I waited, but they do not speak.
They stand there; they answer no more.
translator_notes: The verb hochalti returns from verse 11, forming a frame: Elihu waited, listened, evaluated, and waited again. The friends' silence is now physical: amdu ('they stood still') — they are frozen, speechless, immobilized by their failure. The repetition of lo anu od ('they answered no more') from verse 15 hammers the point: the floor is empty.

verses.text_hebrew: אַעֲנֶ֣ה אַף־אָ֣נִי חֶלְקִ֑י אֲחַוֶּ֖ה דֵּ֝עִ֗י אַף־אָֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: I said, I will answer also my part, I also will shew mine opinion.
verses.rendering:
I too will answer my part.
I too will declare what I know.
translator_notes: The emphatic af ani ('I also, even I') appears twice, framing the verse. The word chelqi ('my part, my portion') suggests Elihu sees the conversation as having allotted shares — each speaker has a portion, and his has not yet been delivered. The verb achavveh ('I will declare') from chavvah appears for the third time (vv. 6, 10, 17), the keynote verb of this poem: Elihu's purpose is to declare, to make known, to set forth.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֭י מָלֵ֣תִי מִלִּ֑ים הֱ֝צִיקַ֗תְנִי ר֣וּחַ בִּטְנִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: For I am full of matter, the spirit within me constraineth me.
verses.rendering:
For I am full of words;
the spirit in my belly presses hard against me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The physical imagery intensifies: maleti millin ('I am full of words') — Elihu is gorged with language, stuffed to capacity. The parallel hetsiqatni ruach bitni ('the spirit/wind of my belly constrains me') uses ruach in its double sense of 'spirit' and 'wind.' The divine spirit that gives understanding (v. 8) is now experienced as trapped gas in the abdomen. This is not crude — it is brilliant. Elihu's inspiration is a physical pressure that demands release. The verb hetsiqatni ('it constrains me, it presses upon me') from tsuq conveys crushing, squeezing force. Elihu is being compressed by his own unspoken wisdom.
translator_notes: The word beten ('belly, womb, inner body') grounds the experience in the gut, not the head. Elihu's wisdom is not intellectual but visceral — it is felt in the body as pressure. This connects to the ancient Near Eastern concept that the internal organs were the seat of thought and emotion.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּ֣ה בִ֭טְנִי כְּיַ֣יִן לֹא־יִפָּתֵ֑חַ כְּאֹב֖וֹת חֲדָשִׁ֣ים יִבָּקֵֽעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, my belly is as wine which hath no vent; it is ready to burst like new bottles.
verses.rendering:
My belly is like wine that has no vent —
like new wineskins, ready to burst.
verses.expanded_rendering: The most vivid image in the speech. Elihu compares himself to a sealed container of fermenting wine: bitni ke-yayin lo yippateach ('my belly is like wine that has not been opened'). New wine produces gas as it ferments; if the wineskin has no outlet (lo yippateach, 'it is not opened'), pressure builds until the skin tears. The parallel ke-ovot chadashim yibbaqa ('like new wineskins it will split open') drives the metaphor to its conclusion — Elihu will either speak or explode. The word ovot ('wineskins') refers to animal-skin containers that expand with fermentation. Elihu's body is the wineskin; his words are the wine; the spirit of God is the fermentation. If he does not open his mouth, he will rupture.
translator_notes: This image will be echoed in Jesus' saying about new wine in old wineskins (Matthew 9:17, Mark 2:22, Luke 5:37-38). The fermentation metaphor implies that Elihu's words are alive, growing, developing pressure of their own — they are not prepared speeches but living insights that demand expression.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲדַבְּרָ֥ה וְיִרְוַח־לִ֑י אֶפְתַּ֖ח שְׂפָתַ֣י וְאֶעֱנֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: I will speak, that I may be refreshed: I will open my lips and answer.
verses.rendering:
Let me speak, so I can breathe.
Let me open my lips and answer.
verses.expanded_rendering: The relief Elihu seeks is ventilation: adabberah ve-yirvach li ('let me speak and let there be relief for me'). The verb yirvach from ravach means 'to be wide, to have space, to breathe freely' — the opposite of the crushing pressure in verse 18. Speaking is breathing. The parallel eftach sefatai ve-e'eneh ('let me open my lips and answer') uses the same verb patach ('to open') that introduced Job's first speech in 3:1. Both men open their mouths under pressure — Job under the pressure of suffering, Elihu under the pressure of inspiration. The word ruach from verse 18 is silently present: the relief (revach) Elihu seeks is literally the release of wind/spirit (ruach). The pun ties the entire speech together.
translator_notes: The shift from 'I am full' (v. 18) to 'let me speak so I can breathe' (v. 20) completes the fermentation metaphor: sealed container, building pressure, release. Elihu's speech is presented not as a choice but as a physical necessity.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־נָ֣א אֶשָּׂ֣א פְנֵי־אִ֑ישׁ וְאֶל־אָ֝דָ֗ם לֹ֣א אֲכַנֶּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Let me not, I pray you, accept any man's person, neither let me give flattering titles unto man.
verses.rendering:
I will not show favoritism to anyone,
and I will not flatter any person.
verses.expanded_rendering: Elihu pledges impartiality: al na essa fenei ish ('let me not lift the face of a man'). The idiom nasa panim ('to lift the face') means to show favor based on status — it is the opposite of the blind justice that Deuteronomy 1:17 demands. Elihu will not defer to Job's former wealth or to the friends' seniority. The parallel ve-el adam lo akhanneh ('and to a human I will not give a flattering title') uses the rare verb kinnah ('to surname, to give an honorific'), suggesting excessive formality or sycophancy. Elihu refuses to soften his words with titles or compliments. He will speak plainly and without social calculation.
translator_notes: This pledge of impartiality echoes the prophetic tradition: Samuel (1 Samuel 12:3), Isaiah, and Amos all claim to speak without favoritism. Elihu positions himself as a truth-teller uncorrupted by social hierarchy. Whether his subsequent speeches live up to this standard is debatable.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֹ֣א יָדַ֣עְתִּי אֲכַנֶּ֑ה כִּ֝מְעַ֗ט יִשָּׂאֵ֥נִי עֹשֵֽׂנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: For I know not to give flattering titles; in so doing my maker would soon take me away.
verses.rendering:
For I do not know how to flatter;
if I did, my Maker would soon carry me off.
verses.expanded_rendering: The final verse grounds Elihu's impartiality in theology, not personality. He says lo yadati akhanneh ('I do not know how to give flattering titles') — this is not a social skill he possesses. But the deeper reason follows: kim'at yissa'eni oseni ('in a short time my Maker would carry me away'). If Elihu flattered, God would kill him. The word oseni ('my Maker') from asah ('to make') is personal and intimate — the God who made Elihu, who formed him, who breathed the spirit into him (v. 8), that same God would destroy him for dishonest speech. Elihu's honesty is not courage; it is survival. He speaks truth because the alternative is death at the hands of his own Creator.
translator_notes: The verb yissa'eni ('would carry me away') from nasa can mean 'to lift up' (in blessing) or 'to carry off' (in judgment). Here it clearly means removal — God would take Elihu out of the conversation, out of the world. This verse establishes the theological stakes of Elihu's speech: he speaks under divine compulsion, and dishonesty would be fatal.
